author: Justin Martyr
interpretation: Justin argues that Psalm 110 cannot refer to Hezekiah or any other king of Judah, since none sat at God's right hand or was called a priest after the order of Melchizedek. The psalm is exclusively messianic. Jesus himself used this argument against the Pharisees, demonstrating that the Messiah must be greater than David.
context: Justin's argument follows the same logic Jesus uses in Matthew 22:41–46. Psalm 110 became the most-quoted Old Testament text in the New Testament and the earliest Christian apologetics.

author: Augustine of Hippo
interpretation: Augustine reads Psalm 110 as proving Christ's dual nature. As David's Lord, he is divine; as David's son, he is human. The session at God's right hand signifies sovereign rule over all creation, exercised now in the church age. The Melchizedekian priesthood supersedes the Levitical order, establishing Christ as both king and priest — a combination forbidden under Mosaic law.
context: Augustine's exposition shaped Western liturgical theology, particularly the understanding of Christ's present heavenly intercession. The king-priest combination became central to Christological reflection on Hebrews.

author: Thomas Aquinas
interpretation: Aquinas systematizes the psalm's theology: the session at God's right hand denotes equality of power with the Father; the scepter from Zion represents the church's extension of Christ's rule; the Melchizedekian priesthood indicates an eternal, non-hereditary priesthood superseding the temporary Aaronic order. Each verse maps to a distinct aspect of Christ's threefold office.
context: Aquinas's systematic treatment of Psalm 110 within his broader Christology helped establish the munus triplex (prophet, priest, king) framework that both Catholic and Reformed theology later adopted.

author: Rashi (Rabbi Shlomo Yitzchaki)
interpretation: Rashi reads Psalm 110 as David's composition about Abraham after his victory over the four kings in Genesis 14. The 'Lord' addressed is Abraham, and the Melchizedek reference connects to the historical encounter between Abraham and the king-priest of Salem. The psalm celebrates Abraham's military triumph, not a future messianic enthronement.
context: Rashi's Abrahamic reading was partly motivated by the need to counter the dominant Christian messianic interpretation. Other rabbinic traditions read the psalm as referring to David himself or to a future messiah, showing diversity within Jewish interpretation.

author: Martin Luther
interpretation: Luther devotes an entire treatise to Psalm 110, calling it the chief psalm of the Messiah. He argues that the psalm establishes Christ's kingdom as a spiritual kingdom of grace, not a political empire. Christ rules through the Word and the sacraments, not through the sword. The 'enemies as footstool' language refers to the subjugation of sin, death, and the devil.
context: Luther's spiritual-kingdom reading of Psalm 110 directly opposed both the papacy's political claims and the Anabaptist movement's theocratic aspirations. It became foundational for the Lutheran two-kingdoms doctrine.

author: Ernst Wilhelm Hengstenberg
interpretation: Hengstenberg defends the Davidic authorship and exclusively messianic reference of Psalm 110 against emerging critical skepticism. He argues that no historical Israelite king could simultaneously be addressed as David's lord and designated as a priest forever. The psalm's language demands a figure who transcends the normal categories of Israelite kingship.
context: Hengstenberg was the leading conservative Old Testament scholar of his generation, systematically defending traditional messianic readings against the Documentary Hypothesis and rationalist criticism.

author: Darrell Bock
interpretation: Bock shows how Peter's Pentecost sermon in Acts 2 uses Psalm 110:1 as the climactic proof that Jesus is both Lord and Messiah. The resurrection and ascension are interpreted as the fulfillment of David's prophecy — Jesus now occupies the position at God's right hand that David foresaw. This enthronement inaugurates the messianic reign that the psalm anticipates.
context: Bock's analysis of how the early church used Psalm 110 demonstrates that it was the interpretive backbone of early Christology. More New Testament passages allude to Psalm 110 than to any other Old Testament text.
